publications.title: Highly divergent T-cell receptor binding modes underlie specific recognition of a bulged viral peptide bound to a human leukocyte antigen class I molecule.
publications.authors: Liu YC, Miles JJ, Neller MA, Gostick E, Price DA, Purcell AW, McCluskey J, Burrows SR, Rossjohn J, Gras S.
publications.executive_summary: Three distinct human TCRs (CA5, SB27, and SB47) were characterized in complex with HLA-B*35:08 presenting a super-bulged viral peptide (LPEPLPQGQLTAY), revealing fundamentally different recognition strategies for the same pMHC-I ligand. While CA5 and SB27 TCRs engaged the peptide and central HLA regions with limited contacts, the SB47 TCR adopted a completely distinct mechanism, bypassing the bulged peptide entirely and binding to the extreme N-terminal end of HLA-B*35:08 through an atypical footprint including previously unobserved TCR contact sites. These divergent binding modes, despite targeting identical antigen-presenting surfaces, highlight how TCR recognition of bulged peptides depends on unpredictable structural strategies and emphasize the plasticity of the TCR-pMHC interface. The finding that the same HLA-peptide combination can be recognized via multiple alternative docking angles helps explain both the specificity and cross-reactivity patterns observed among TCRs, with implications for understanding how the T-cell repertoire generates recognition diversity for unconventional peptide presentations.
